name: Aethra
shortDescription: Aethra was a princess of Troezen and the mother of Theseus by either King Aegeus of Athens or the god Poseidon.
description: Aethra plays a significant role in the myth of Theseus. She was the daughter of Pittheus, the king of Troezen. Her most notable connection is to Theseus, the great Athenian hero. According to the most popular version of the myth, Aegeus, King of Athens, visited Troezen seeking a cure for his childlessness. Pittheus, understanding the oracle's prophecy, arranged for Aegeus to become drunk and sleep with Aethra. However, another account claims that Poseidon, the god of the sea, also lay with Aethra on the same night, making Theseus of dual paternity – both mortal and divine. Before leaving Troezen, Aegeus placed his sword and sandals under a large rock, instructing Aethra that when their son was strong enough to lift the rock, he should retrieve them and travel to Athens to claim his birthright. Aethra raised Theseus in secret. Later in her life, Aethra became a handmaiden to Helen of Troy and was taken to Troy by Paris. After the fall of Troy, she was rescued by her grandsons, Demophon and Acamas, the sons of Theseus. Her story highlights themes of divine parentage, hidden identity, and the heroic journey. She represents the maternal figure who nurtures a hero and facilitates his destiny.

sources.title: The Lives of the Noble Grecians and Romans
sources.author: Plutarch

keyMyths.title: The Conception of Theseus
keyMyths.description: Aethra conceived Theseus either through Aegeus, King of Athens, or Poseidon, the god of the sea, or both. Aegeus left his sword and sandals under a rock, instructing Aethra to send their son to Athens when he was strong enough to retrieve them, thus setting the stage for Theseus's heroic journey.

keyMyths.title: The Lifting of the Rock
keyMyths.description: Aethra kept the secret of Theseus's parentage until he was old enough and strong enough to lift the rock concealing Aegeus's tokens. This act proved Theseus's strength and worthiness, prompting him to embark on the perilous journey to Athens to claim his birthright.

keyMyths.title: Aethra as Helen's Handmaiden
keyMyths.description: Later in life, Aethra became a slave and handmaiden to Helen of Troy, abducted along with her by Paris. This misfortune brought her to Troy, where she remained until the city's fall.
keyMyths.source: Homer, Iliad (indirectly implied through later retellings)

keyMyths.title: Rescue by her Grandsons
keyMyths.description: After the Trojan War, Aethra was rescued from slavery by her grandsons, Demophon and Acamas, the sons of Theseus. This act of filial piety underscores the enduring importance of family ties and the eventual triumph of justice.

extendedContent.title: Aethra's Role in Theseus' Conception and Early Life
extendedContent.content:
Aethra's primary significance stems from her role in the birth of Theseus, a pivotal figure in Athenian mythology. Aegeus, the childless King of Athens, sought advice from the Oracle at Delphi regarding his inability to produce an heir. The ambiguous prophecy he received led him to Troezen, where he was hosted by Pittheus, Aethra's father. Pittheus, known for his wisdom and understanding, interpreted the oracle's meaning and orchestrated a meeting between Aegeus and Aethra. 

According to the prevalent narrative, Aegeus, after a night with Aethra, instructed her that if she bore a son, she should raise him in secret. He then placed his sword and sandals under a large rock, telling Aethra that when their son was strong enough to lift the rock and retrieve these items, she should send him to Athens. This act served as a test of the boy's strength and ensured his rightful claim to the Athenian throne. Some accounts suggest that Poseidon, the god of the sea, also lay with Aethra on the same night, leading to speculation about Theseus' divine lineage. This duality adds complexity to Theseus' character, linking him to both mortal and divine realms.

Aethra diligently followed Aegeus's instructions, raising Theseus in Troezen. She concealed his true parentage, allowing him to grow up unaware of his royal heritage. She instilled in him values of courage, strength, and wisdom, preparing him for the challenges that lay ahead. The secrecy surrounding Theseus's birth and upbringing added to the mystique surrounding his eventual arrival in Athens and his claim to the throne. Her careful guidance and protection were instrumental in shaping the future hero.

extendedContent.title: Aethra's Abduction and Later Life
extendedContent.content:
Aethra's story doesn't end with Theseus's departure for Athens. She reappears later in mythological accounts, often as a victim of abduction and enslavement. During Theseus's absence, she was abducted by Castor and Pollux, the Dioscuri, as retaliation for Theseus's abduction of their sister Helen. This event highlights the tumultuous nature of the heroic age and the constant threat of violence and upheaval.

She was then taken to Lacedaemon (Sparta) and served as a slave to Helen. This period marks a significant change in Aethra's life, transitioning from a royal figure to a captive. Her enslavement underscores the vulnerability of even noble women in a society dominated by warfare and power struggles. It's a stark contrast to her earlier role as the secret guardian of a future king.

After the Trojan War, Aethra was recognized by her grandsons, Demophon and Acamas, the sons of Theseus, during the sack of Troy. They rescued her and brought her back to Athens. This reunion signifies a return to her rightful place and a restoration of her honor. Her rescue by her grandsons highlights the enduring bonds of family and the eventual triumph of justice. While her later life is less detailed in surviving accounts, her return to Athens likely brought her a measure of peace and recognition for her role in the lineage of Theseus.
